events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Chen. She is a never-smoker with no significant tobacco or alcohol history. She works as a high school principal. She reports a history of a prior HPV infection diagnosed approximately 15 years ago. She has no family history of head and neck cancer. Medical history is notable for well-controlled hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) and mild gastroesophageal reflux disease. She is of East Asian (Taiwanese-American) descent. Performance status ECOG 0 at presentation.

At age 52 years, the patient was diagnosed with squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue (oropharynx), HPV-positive (p16+), AJCC 8th edition stage III (T2N1M0). Primary site: Base of Tongue (ICD-O-3 site code C01.9). Histology: Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS (ICD-O-3 histology code 8070/3). TNM: T2 (primary tumor 2.8 cm in greatest dimension, limited to oropharynx), N1 (single ipsilateral cervical lymph node 2.5 cm, level II-III left neck), M0 (no distant metastasis). Summary stage: Regional. HPV status: Positive by p16 immunohistochemistry (strong, diffuse staining >70% cells). Tumor grade: Poorly differentiated. Tobacco history: Never-smoker. Alcohol history: Social drinker, <1 drink/week. Pack-year history: 0. Confirmation: Positive histology (core needle biopsy of left level IIA lymph node and direct laryngoscopy biopsy of base of tongue). Tumor size: 28 mm. Laterality: Left. Initial presenting symptom: 3-month history of left-sided throat discomfort and odynophagia, noticed an enlarged left neck lymph node.

At age 52, the patient underwent biopsy of a left level IIA cervical lymph node (core needle biopsy) and direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of the base of tongue lesion. Pathology report type: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Left level IIA lymph node core biopsy: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated, with associated cystic change; p16 immunohistochemistry strongly and diffusely positive (>90% of tumor cells); EBER in situ hybridization negative (ruling out EBV-associated carcinoma). Base of tongue biopsy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated, with prominent lymphocytic infiltrate (tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes); no perineural invasion identified on biopsy specimen; p16 strongly positive. Ki-67 proliferative index approximately 75%. PD-L1 expression by 22C3 assay: TPS 40%, CPS 65.

At age 52, the patient had an MRI of the neck with contrast (dedicated head and neck protocol), which showed cancer. Primary site: 2.8 x 2.1 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass arising from the left base of tongue, extending to the left vallecula but not crossing midline; no involvement of the epiglottis or pre-epiglottic space; no deep space invasion into the intrinsic tongue musculature beyond the oropharynx. Nodal disease: Single enlarged left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.5 x 2.0 cm with central necrosis and irregular enhancement, consistent with nodal metastasis; no extranodal extension identified; no bilateral or contralateral adenopathy. No involvement of the carotid artery. No skull base invasion. Metastatic sites: None identified. MRI findings consistent with T2N1M0 oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue primary.

At age 52, the patient had a PET-CT (FDG PET/CT, skull base to mid-thigh), which showed cancer. Findings: Intensely FDG-avid left base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2), corresponding to known primary tumor. Single intensely FDG-avid left level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 11.8, size 2.5 cm), consistent with nodal metastasis. No contralateral or bilateral cervical nodal involvement. No mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular adenopathy. No distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No synchronous primary identified. Lungs: Clear without nodules or masses. Liver: No focal lesions. Bones: No suspicious osseous lesions. Impression: FDG-avid left oropharyngeal primary and single left level IIA nodal metastasis; no evidence of distant metastatic disease; consistent with stage III (T2N1M0) HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma per AJCC 8th edition.

At age 52, the patient had next-generation sequencing (comprehensive genomic profiling) performed for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-positive, based on a specimen obtained from the left level IIA cervical lymph node (formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded core biopsy tissue). Sequencing platform: 500+ gene panel with DNA and RNA sequencing. Results: HPV16 integration detected (positive for HPV-16 E6/E7 oncoproteins by RNA expression; high viral load). Somatic mutations: PIK3CA p.E545K (exon 9, activating helical domain mutation; allele frequency 28%, likely pathogenic, potentially actionable); TRAF3 p.Q53* (frameshift, loss of function, allele frequency 22%, consistent with known HPV+ HNSCC biology - TRAF3 loss promotes NF-kB activation); FAT1 p.E3077* (loss of function, allele frequency 19%); NOTCH1 p.R2328W (allele frequency 16%, variant of uncertain significance). Copy number alterations: 3q26 amplification (including PIK3CA locus, consistent with PIK3CA gain); 11q13 amplification (CCND1 region, low level); chromosome 3p deletion (including FHIT). Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected. CDKN2A: No mutation or homozygous deletion detected (intact p16/CDKN2A, consistent with HPV-driven carcinogenesis). TP53: No mutation detected (wild-type, consistent with HPV+ HNSCC). RB1: No pathogenic mutation detected. KRAS, HRAS, NRAS: All wild-type. EGFR: No mutation or amplification. TMB: 5.2 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: Microsatellite stable. PD-L1 CPS: 65 (as noted on IHC). Germline analysis: No pathogenic germline variants identified in cancer predisposition genes (BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D).

At age 52, the patient had an initial multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) consultation at the head and neck oncology clinic. Cancer present. Assessment: 52-year-old never-smoking female with newly diagnosed HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, AJCC 8th edition stage III (T2N1M0). Given her excellent performance status (ECOG 0), never-smoker status, and favorable HPV-positive biology, she has an excellent prognosis with definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy. Treatment recommendation: Definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT). Tumor board discussed de-escalation trial enrollment (ECOG-ACRIN EA3161 or similar); patient counseled on de-escalation vs. standard-of-care options. Patient declined enrollment in a de-escalation trial at this time and opted for standard-of-care definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy. Dental evaluation completed; 2 carious teeth extracted prior to radiation. Audiology baseline assessment: normal bilateral hearing. Speech and swallowing baseline evaluation completed (modified barium swallow: normal swallowing function at baseline). Nutrition consult completed; baseline weight 62 kg, BMI 22.4. Fertility counseling not applicable. PEG tube discussed prophylactically given anticipated mucositis severity; patient agreed to prophylactic PEG placement.

At age 52, the patient underwent prophylactic percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement in anticipation of treatment-related dysphagia and mucositis. Procedure performed by interventional gastroenterology under conscious sedation. Indication: Prophylactic nutritional support tube placement prior to definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma; patient was counseled that grade 3+ mucositis would likely necessitate enteral feeding during and after radiation. Procedure: Uncomplicated PEG tube placement (20 Fr). Post-procedure recovery uneventful. Tube feeds initiated at low rate and advanced to goal rate over 2 days for familiarization. Patient continued oral intake at baseline at time of placement.

At age 52, the patient initiated definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (left base of tongue, T2N1M0). Radiation type: IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. Treatment intent: Curative/definitive. Radiation dose and fractionation: High-risk PTV (primary tumor and involved nodal regions): 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction) over 7 weeks. Intermediate-risk PTV (elective bilateral neck including contralateral at-risk nodal stations): 63 Gy in 35 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction). Low-risk PTV (bilateral elective neck, levels II-IV): 56 Gy in 35 fractions (1.6 Gy/fraction). Concurrent cisplatin administered weekly (40 mg/m2 IV weekly x7 cycles) rather than high-dose tri-weekly regimen, per institutional preference and to potentially reduce late toxicity. Treatment volumes: Primary left base of tongue and left levels IB, IIA, IIB, III, IV, VA; right levels IIA, IIB, III, IV electively included. Organs at risk constraints met: Spinal cord max 44 Gy; brainstem max 52 Gy; mean parotid gland (right, contralateral) 22 Gy; mandible max 70 Gy; larynx mean 38 Gy; pharyngeal constrictor muscles optimized to reduce late dysphagia. Setup: Aquaplast thermoplastic mask immobilization; daily CBCT image guidance.

At age 52, the patient initiated concurrent cisplatin chemotherapy administered weekly during definitive IMRT for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Regimen: Cisplatin 40 mg/m2 IV weekly x 7 planned cycles (weeks 1-7 of radiation). Dose: 40 mg/m2 (BSA 1.62 m2; dose = 64.8 mg, rounded to 65 mg). Intent: Curative, radiosensitization. Pre-medications: Dexamethasone 12 mg IV, ondansetron 8 mg IV, fosaprepitant 150 mg IV, magnesium sulfate 2g IV, and aggressive IV hydration (1 L NS pre and post cisplatin). Cycle 1 administered without complication. Baseline creatinine 0.7 mg/dL (CrCl estimated 95 mL/min). Audiologic monitoring planned before and after treatment.

At age 52, during week 3 of concurrent chemoradiotherapy, the patient developed grade 2 mucositis (patchy pseudomembranous mucositis of the oropharynx and base of tongue) with associated odynophagia. She was started on magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine/lidocaine/Maalox), oral rinses with salt and baking soda, and scheduled acetaminophen. Oral intake was significantly reduced but maintained above 50% of nutritional needs. PEG tube feeding was initiated at a supplemental rate (50% of caloric needs via tube, remainder orally). Weight at this point: 60.1 kg (down 1.9 kg from baseline). Cisplatin cycle 3 administered with dose reduction deferred; full dose administered (creatinine stable at 0.8 mg/dL).

At age 52, during week 5 of concurrent chemoradiotherapy, the patient developed grade 3 mucositis (confluent pseudomembranous mucositis involving more than one-third of the mucosal surface, with severe pain requiring continuous opioid analgesia). She was transitioned to full PEG tube feedings (100% of caloric and fluid needs via tube) as oral intake became negligible. Pain management escalated: long-acting oxycodone 10 mg twice daily with immediate-release oxycodone 5 mg every 4 hours as needed. Oral care regimen intensified. Additionally, the patient developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck (moist desquamation in skin folds). Topical betamethasone and moisture barrier cream applied to affected areas. Weight: 58.2 kg (down 3.8 kg from baseline, approximately 6% weight loss). Cisplatin cycle 5 administered at full dose; magnesium 1.5 mEq/L (grade 1 hypomagnesemia), supplemented with oral magnesium. Neutrophil count 1.8 x10^9/L (borderline, no growth factor required as no fever).

At age 52, during week 6 of concurrent chemoradiotherapy, the patient developed grade 2 xerostomia (moderate dry mouth, requiring frequent liquid intake to aid mastication/swallowing). She also developed grade 2 dysgeusia (significantly altered taste). Grade 3 dysphagia continued with full PEG dependence. Additionally noted: Grade 1 fatigue persisting throughout treatment. Cisplatin cycle 6 was held at week 6 due to a creatinine rise to 1.2 mg/dL (from baseline 0.7 mg/dL), representing a >1.5-fold increase (grade 1-2 nephrotoxicity). Patient was aggressively hydrated with IV fluids (2L NS over 4 hours), and cisplatin was rescheduled for the following week. No grade 3-4 nephrotoxicity. Audiogram performed: Bilateral high-frequency hearing threshold shift of 10-15 dB at 4 kHz (grade 1 ototoxicity by CTCAE).

At age 52, the patient completed week 7 (final week) of definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy. Cancer present (active treatment ongoing). Treatment summary: Patient completed 70 Gy IMRT in 35 fractions over 7 weeks. Cisplatin administered for 6 of 7 planned weekly cycles (cycle 6 delayed by 1 week and administered in week 7; no cycle during the week of delay; cumulative cisplatin dose: 390 mg/m2 total across 6 cycles). Reason for dose omission of one cycle: transient nephrotoxicity (creatinine elevation) as noted. End-of-treatment toxicity assessment: Grade 3 mucositis (ongoing, expected to worsen over 1-2 weeks post-treatment); grade 3 dysphagia (PEG dependent); grade 2 radiation dermatitis (bilateral neck, resolving); grade 2 xerostomia; grade 2 dysgeusia; grade 1 fatigue; grade 1 hypomagnesemia; grade 1 ototoxicity. Weight at end of treatment: 57.0 kg (down 5 kg from baseline, 8.1% weight loss). Patient tolerating PEG tube feeds well, maintaining nutrition and hydration via tube. No hospitalizations during treatment. Plans: Acute follow-up in 1 week; continue PEG tube feeds until oral intake recovers; pain management weaning as mucositis resolves; re-imaging planned at 8-12 weeks post-treatment.

At age 52, approximately 2 weeks post-completion of chemoradiotherapy, the patient presented for urgent evaluation with worsening mucositis pain (grade 3 persisting), inability to swallow saliva, and dehydration. She was admitted to the hospital for 3 days for IV hydration, pain management optimization, and supportive care. During admission: IV opioids titrated and transitioned to oral/PEG route; IV magnesium replaced (Mg 1.4 mEq/L); electrolytes monitored and repleted; mucositis wound care. Creatinine normalized to 0.75 mg/dL with hydration. No evidence of infection. Patient discharged with: PEG tube feeds at goal rate, oxycodone taper plan, outpatient speech and swallowing therapy referral, and close follow-up in head and neck oncology clinic in 1 week.

At age 52, approximately 6 weeks post-completion of chemoradiotherapy, the patient was seen in follow-up. Cancer status indeterminate (too early for definitive restaging). Mucositis: Resolving; grade 1 (erythema only, no pseudomembranes). Pain: Significantly improved; opioids successfully tapered to as-needed acetaminophen and ibuprofen only. Oral intake: Resuming soft foods and thick liquids; approximately 40-50% of caloric needs met orally; PEG tube supplementing remaining nutrition. Weight: 57.8 kg (slight recovery). Xerostomia: Persistent grade 2; using artificial saliva spray and frequent water sips. Skin: Grade 1 radiation dermatitis, nearly resolved; mild hyperpigmentation. Neck: Clinically, the left neck lymph node is no longer palpable. Swallowing therapy: Progressing with exercises; modified barium swallow scheduled. Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low normal, continuing oral supplementation). Plan: Continue PEG supplementation, advance diet as tolerated, reimage at 10-12 weeks post-treatment per protocol.

At age 52 (approximately 12 weeks post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient had a PET-CT (FDG PET/CT, skull base to mid-thigh) for post-treatment response assessment, which showed no cancer. Findings: No FDG-avid disease at the left base of tongue primary site; the region shows post-treatment changes with mild increased background uptake (SUVmax 2.1) consistent with treatment effect/inflammation, not malignancy. Previously identified left level IIA lymph node: No longer FDG-avid; small residual soft tissue density (8 mm) with SUVmax 1.8, consistent with treatment effect and fibrosis, not viable tumor. No new FDG-avid cervical, mediastinal, or distant metastatic disease identified. No locoregional recurrence. No distant metastases. Impression: Complete metabolic response (CMR) to concurrent chemoradiotherapy for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No evidence of residual or recurrent malignancy.

At age 52 (approximately 12 weeks post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient had an MRI of the neck with contrast (post-treatment surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: Left base of tongue: Post-treatment changes; no residual discrete mass or abnormal enhancement to suggest residual tumor; mild mucosal thickening consistent with radiation-induced changes. Left level IIA lymph node: Markedly decreased in size (now 8 mm, previously 25 mm); no internal necrosis; no abnormal enhancement; consistent with complete nodal response. No new cervical lymphadenopathy. Pharyngeal/laryngeal anatomy intact; no evidence of radiation necrosis. Carotid arteries patent bilaterally. Soft tissue planes intact. Impression: Complete radiographic response to definitive chemoradiotherapy; no evidence of residual or recurrent disease.

At age 52 (12 weeks post-treatment), the patient was seen in the head and neck oncology clinic for post-treatment response assessment. Cancer absent (complete response documented by PET-CT and MRI). Complete response confirmed by imaging. Assessment: Complete metabolic and radiographic response to definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (T2N1M0). Excellent oncologic outcome consistent with favorable HPV-positive biology in a never-smoker. No evidence of disease (NED). Discussion of surveillance schedule: Physical examination and nasopharyngoscopy every 1-3 months for year 1, every 2-4 months for year 2, every 4-6 months for years 3-5, then annually. Annual chest CT/imaging per institutional protocol. TSH monitoring (risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism). Audiology follow-up. Late toxicity management: Xerostomia (grade 2, ongoing - prescribed pilocarpine 5 mg three times daily); PEG tube still in place (oral intake approximately 70% of needs; plan to remove PEG when patient reaches 80-90% oral intake and weight stable). Swallowing therapy ongoing. Discussion of de-escalation: Patient was counseled that had she been enrolled in a de-escalation trial (e.g., reduced-dose cisplatin or radiation dose reduction), she likely would have had an equally favorable outcome given her complete response and favorable biology. This information documented for future reference but no adjuvant de-escalation intervention undertaken (treatment is complete).

At age 52 (approximately 5 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient underwent elective PEG tube removal following successful recovery of oral intake. Oral intake had recovered to approximately 85% of caloric needs by mouth over the preceding 6 weeks, with weight stable at 59.5 kg (recovery to within 4% of pre-treatment baseline). Swallowing therapy had progressed to a regular soft diet with thin liquids. Modified barium swallow performed 2 weeks prior demonstrated mild pharyngeal dysmotility with trace aspiration on thin liquids only; patient placed on modified diet with nectar-thick liquids and swallowing precautions. PEG removal performed endoscopically without complication under conscious sedation. Patient tolerating full nutrition orally at the time of PEG removal.

At age 52 (6 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient was seen for surveillance follow-up. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease. Physical examination: Oropharynx and base of tongue without visible or palpable tumor. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: Mucosa of base of tongue and oropharynx appears post-radiation; no mucosal irregularity, ulceration, or mass suggestive of recurrence. Larynx: Normal appearance and motion. Late toxicity update: Xerostomia grade 1-2 (improved on pilocarpine; patient finds it manageable); dysgeusia grade 1 (some taste improvement, but still altered compared to baseline); mild trismus grade 1 (jaw-opening exercises prescribed, mouth opening 35 mm, previously 42 mm); dysphagia grade 1 on regular soft diet with nectar-thick liquids; no PEG dependence. TSH: 4.8 mIU/L (high normal; thyroid function being monitored). Weight: 60.2 kg (essentially recovered to baseline). ECOG performance status: 1 (mild activity limitation due to xerostomia and fatigue). Quality of life discussion: Patient reports significant impact on social eating and professional activities (school functions). Referred to survivorship clinic. Plan: Continue pilocarpine, swallowing therapy, trismus exercises; TSH recheck in 3 months; next imaging surveillance with MRI neck at 12 months post-treatment.

At age 53 (approximately 10 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient developed symptomatic hypothyroidism with TSH rising to 12.4 mIU/L and free T4 0.6 ng/dL, consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism (a common late effect of neck irradiation). Symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain (2 kg over 2 months), constipation, and cold intolerance. Grade 2 radiation-induced hypothyroidism by CTCAE. Management: Levothyroxine dose increased (pre-existing levothyroxine dose 50 mcg/day increased to 100 mcg/day; prior hypothyroidism on stable low-dose levothyroxine at baseline, now requiring substantially higher dose due to radiation-induced additional thyroid injury). Repeat TSH at 6-8 weeks planned to confirm adequate replacement. Endocrinology consultation placed.

At age 53 (approximately 12 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient had an MRI of the neck with contrast (annual surveillance imaging), which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of locoregional recurrence at the left base of tongue primary site or in the cervical lymph node stations. Post-treatment changes at the left base of tongue: Stable mucosal thickening and mild enhancement consistent with known radiation changes; no new masses, ulceration, or abnormal enhancement to suggest recurrence. Previously noted left level IIA nodal residual (8 mm) has further decreased to 5 mm and is not FDG-avid on concurrent PET component. No new cervical adenopathy. Carotid arteries: Mild atherosclerotic changes bilaterally without significant stenosis; radiation-induced vascular changes noted. No evidence of radiation necrosis of mandible or soft tissues. Impression: No evidence of locoregional recurrence at 12 months post-treatment; stable post-treatment changes.

At age 53 (12 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient was seen for annual surveillance. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 12 months post-treatment. Oncologic status: NED (no evidence of disease). Physical examination: Oropharynx without palpable or visible recurrence. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: No mucosal lesion or mass; post-radiation mucosal changes stable. Imaging: Annual MRI neck negative for recurrence (as noted above). Late toxicity profile at 12 months: Xerostomia grade 1 (well-managed with pilocarpine and oral hydration; significantly improved from treatment period); dysgeusia grade 1 (partial taste recovery; approximately 60% of normal taste function); trismus improving with jaw exercises (mouth opening 40 mm); dysphagia grade 1 (tolerating regular diet with thin liquids; occasional coughing with large liquid boluses; continuing swallowing exercises); radiation-induced hypothyroidism managed on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day (TSH now 2.1 mIU/L, well-controlled); mild carotid bruit detected on exam (referred to vascular surgery for carotid duplex ultrasound surveillance). No evidence of osteoradionecrosis. Dental: Fluoride treatments ongoing, no dental extractions post-radiation needed. Quality of life: Significantly improved from treatment period; returned to full-time work as principal. ECOG performance status 0. Plan: Continue surveillance every 3-4 months; chest imaging at 18 months; continue survivorship follow-up; de-escalation retrospective discussion - oncologist notes that retrospectively, given her complete response and favorable biology (HPV+, never-smoker, T2N1M0), she would have been an excellent candidate for a de-escalation protocol; this is noted in the chart for clinical context.

At age 53 (18 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient had a CT chest without contrast (surveillance imaging for distant metastasis and secondary primary detection), which showed no cancer. Findings: No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations suggestive of metastatic disease or primary lung malignancy. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Pleural spaces clear. Bones: No lytic or blastic lesions. Liver (partially imaged): No focal lesions. Impression: No evidence of thoracic metastatic disease or secondary primary lung cancer at 18 months post-treatment for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

At age 53 (18 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient was seen for surveillance. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 18 months. Interval history: No new symptoms; no neck masses or throat pain. Continued improvement in quality of life. Xerostomia: Grade 1, well-tolerated; pilocarpine dose optimized. Swallowing: Tolerating regular diet and thin liquids without aspiration symptoms. Fatigue: Resolved. Hearing: Repeat audiogram at 18 months shows stable bilateral high-frequency threshold shift (4 kHz) consistent with grade 1 ototoxicity, no progression, no intervention required. Thyroid: TSH 1.9 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day (well-controlled). Carotid duplex ultrasound result (performed at 14 months): Mild bilateral carotid intimal-medial thickening without hemodynamically significant stenosis; annual surveillance recommended by vascular surgery. Dental: Fluoride use ongoing, no osteoradionecrosis. The patient was counseled on the excellent prognosis associated with HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma in never-smokers with complete response and continues to have an excellent prognosis. Continued NED status. Plan: Surveillance every 4-6 months; annual MRI neck and chest imaging per protocol; continue late effects monitoring.

At age 54 (24 months post-chemoradiotherapy), the patient had an MRI of the neck with contrast (2-year surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of locoregional recurrence. Post-treatment mucosal changes at the left base of tongue are stable compared to prior MRI. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Small previously noted left level IIA soft tissue residual has resolved completely (no longer detectable). Radiation-related soft tissue changes in the neck stable. No radiation-induced necrosis. Carotid arteries: Stable mild atherosclerotic changes without significant interval progression. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or new malignancy at 24 months post-treatment; stable post-treatment changes consistent with complete response.

At age 54 (24 months post-chemoradiotherapy, 2-year survivorship visit), the patient was seen for surveillance and survivorship assessment. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 2 years. Two-year survivorship assessment: The patient has achieved and maintained a complete response to definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (T2N1M0). She remains NED at 2 years. Late toxicity summary at 2 years: Xerostomia grade 1 (mild, well-managed, significantly improved from treatment period; ongoing pilocarpine use); no clinically significant dysphagia (tolerating regular diet with thin liquids; minor residual pharyngeal dysmotility on functional assessment); trismus resolved (mouth opening 41 mm, within 1 mm of pre-treatment baseline); hypothyroidism controlled on levothyroxine; grade 1 ototoxicity (bilateral high-frequency hearing loss, stable, no hearing aids required). No osteoradionecrosis. No second primary identified. Quality of life: Patient reports excellent quality of life and has fully resumed professional and social activities. Minor residual xerostomia and taste alteration are the predominant ongoing complaints. She reports feeling 'essentially back to normal.' ECOG performance status 0. Dental health maintained. Psychosocial: Patient has participated in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group and reports good psychological adjustment. HPV vaccination: Discussion regarding adolescent children's HPV vaccination status - counseled to ensure completion of HPV vaccine series. Plan: Transition to less frequent surveillance (every 6 months for years 3-5); annual MRI neck and chest CT; continue thyroid monitoring; continue carotid surveillance; continue late effects management with survivorship team. Oncologist's note regarding de-escalation: This patient's outstanding outcome (complete response, durable NED at 2 years, early toxicity managed without treatment interruption, acceptable late effects) underscores the excellent prognosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma in never-smokers. Retrospective analysis suggests she may have achieved equivalent tumor control with a reduced-intensity regimen; this case will be included in institutional de-escalation research data.

scenario_blurb: HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (base of tongue) stage III (T2N1M0) in a 52-year-old nonsmoker. Treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin and intensity-modulated radiation therapy. Develops grade 3 mucositis and requires PEG tube for nutrition support. Achieves complete response. Post-treatment surveillance with consideration of de-escalation in the adjuvant setting.
